steps.title: Connect the two texts
steps.content: Text 1 says King's Cross suggests infinity and eternal adventure to Margaret, not ordinary material prosperity. Text 2 explains why that matters in Howards End: London represents capitalism and material wealth, while the countryside, reached through King's Cross, represents idealism, tradition, authentic personal connection, the aesthetic, and the infinite.

steps.title: Reject materialist readings
steps.content: Choice B reverses the relationship by making King's Cross a sign of materialism intruding into rural space. Choice C invents Margaret's disillusionment, even though Text 1 presents the station as opening onto infinity and adventure. Choice D treats the station's plainness as proof of capitalist dominance, which neither text supports.

steps.title: Choose the idealist reading
steps.content: Choice A fits both texts because it presents King's Cross as a connection between London and the countryside and links it to intangible abundance and authentic engagements. That matches Text 1's infinity language and Text 2's explanation of the countryside's idealist values. The answer is A.
